content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most teams still talk about AI coding as if the main variable is the prompt.
The prompt matters. So do the model, the IDE, and the tool choice. But once AI starts writing a meaningful amount of production code, the bigger problem is not prompt quality. It is workflow state.
That is the part of AI-assisted development that gets underestimated.
The code is no longer moving through one developer’s local workflow from editor to tests to pull request. It is moving through a system made up of the editor, the repository, generated files, test results, CI pipelines, architecture rules, and review decisions. Each of those holds part of the truth about the change. None of them fully describe it on their own.
That is why AI coding failures often do not look like “the model wrote bad code.” They look like the workflow lost track of itself.
The agent updated the files it saw, but missed the migration. The tests passed locally, but CI failed because a generated artifact was stale. The diff looked complete, but the branch still violated a package boundary or a build assumption. The code looked done before the workflow was actually done.
That is the real problem.
AI coding is becoming a state-coordination problem disguised as a prompt problem.
Context is not state
AI coding tools are built around context. They read files, infer intent, and use the surrounding repository to produce a plausible change. That is useful, but it creates a false sense of confidence because context is not the same thing as workflow state.
Context is what the model can see.
State is what the repository actually is after the change.
Those are not equivalent.
An agent can have enough context to update an API handler and still miss the migration that should accompany it. It can refactor a shared type and still fail to update a downstream consumer. It can generate tests that pass locally while leaving a codegen step unfinished or a CI-only build path broken.
The model may have enough information to make a good edit. It still may not know whether the repository, as a system, is coherent after that edit.
That gap matters more than most prompt advice does.
If you want a useful mental model for AI-assisted development, it is this:

• Context helps the model produce a change.

• Workflow state determines whether that change actually belongs in the codebase.
Those are different responsibilities, and only one of them lives inside the prompt.
The repository is now a multi-writer system
Before AI coding, the repository was already touched by more than one actor. Developers wrote code, CI ran checks, codegen tools updated artifacts, and reviewers requested changes. But the dominant writer was still the human developer.
That is changing.
Now a single feature may involve:

• a developer writing part of the implementation

• an IDE agent editing files

• a terminal-based coding agent running commands and generating tests

• code generation steps updating clients or schemas

• CI validating the branch

• a reviewer making follow-up edits after the agent finishes
That is not one linear workflow anymore. It is a multi-writer system.
And once the repo becomes a multi-writer system, familiar engineering problems show up quickly. One actor changes a file another actor assumed was stable. A generated artifact drifts from the source change. A branch passes local tests but fails in the canonical pipeline. The final state that gets merged is not exactly the state any one participant evaluated.
This is where a lot of AI coding pain actually comes from.
The problem is not only whether the model can write correct code. The problem is that no single participant naturally owns the full state of the change anymore.
If you have worked on systems where state is fragmented across services, this pattern should feel familiar. I wrote about a similar coordination problem from a Web3 angle in Why Web3 Apps Are Finally Starting to Feel Normal. The domains are different, but the engineering shape is similar: once state is spread across systems you do not fully control, reliability becomes a coordination problem.
Why AI-generated code looks complete before the workflow is complete
This is the most dangerous part of AI coding because the failure is subtle.
The code often looks done before the workflow is actually done.
The route exists. The component renders. The tests the agent added pass. The diff is clean enough that a reviewer can imagine merging it. But the branch is still incomplete in ways the code itself does not reveal.
Maybe a migration is missing. Maybe the generated client was never rebuilt. Maybe an integration test in another package now fails. Maybe the branch depends on an environment variable that only CI exposes. Maybe the change technically works, but violates a repository rule nobody encoded outside tribal memory.
This is one reason AI-generated changes can feel both impressive and fragile at the same time. They arrive with the shape of a finished answer. That shape is persuasive. It lowers the reviewer’s guard even when the surrounding workflow state has not been reconciled.
Human developers also ship incomplete changes, of course. But they usually know what they skipped, what they did not verify, or which part of the repo they never touched. AI-generated changes often fail differently: they look self-contained even when the surrounding system state is not.
That is why teams can feel more productive while simultaneously becoming less certain about what is actually safe to merge.
Tests and CI are now reconciliation layers
Once AI becomes part of implementation, tests and CI stop being just quality checks. They become reconciliation layers.
Their job is no longer only “does the code pass.” It becomes “does the branch still make sense as a repository state after all the generated changes, side effects, and assumptions are accounted for?”
That is a much bigger responsibility.
Modern CI systems already sit at the center of the repository workflow. GitHub Actions and GitLab CI/CD both frame CI as automated workflows that build, test, and validate changes in response to repository events. In an AI-assisted workflow, that role becomes more important because CI is often the first place where the branch is evaluated against the repo’s real rules instead of the model’s internal picture of the task.
This is also why AI coding tends to expose weak engineering workflows very quickly.
If your tests are shallow, if CI is flaky, if code generation is loosely enforced, or if repository rules live mostly in team memory, AI will amplify those weaknesses. You will get more code faster, but you will also get more incomplete changes that look finished until the workflow catches up with them.
That is why I think the interesting AI coding question is not “which prompt got the best output?” It is “which system tells us whether the change is actually complete?”
The answer, most of the time, is not the prompt window. It is the verification layer around the repository.
That is also why I keep coming back to the idea that CI is becoming the practical backend of AI-assisted development. I broke that argument down more directly in a separate piece, Why CI Becomes the Real Backend for AI-Assisted Development, because once code generation becomes cheap, the system that validates and reconciles repository state becomes much more important than the system that merely produces code.
Better prompts help. Better workflow boundaries help more
This is not an argument against better prompting.
Clear prompts matter. Good project instructions matter. Better context retrieval matters. Strong task framing helps a lot.
But teams are increasingly making a category mistake. They are trying to solve a workflow-state problem with better prompt wording.
That only goes so far.
If the repository depends on generated artifacts being in sync, that rule should not live only in a prompt. If schema changes require migrations, that should be enforced by the workflow. If package boundaries matter, the repo should know that. If a branch is not valid until certain tests, builds, or checks pass, those invariants should be executable, not just implied.
The more your workflow depends on the model remembering hidden project rules, the more fragile the system becomes.
The more those rules are encoded in the repository’s verification layers, the more resilient the workflow becomes.
That is the shift teams need to make. The goal is not to teach the model every hidden truth about the codebase. The goal is to build a workflow where the codebase can defend itself when the model does not know those truths.
AI coding is exposing an engineering problem, not just a tooling problem
This is why I think the AI coding conversation is still too narrow.
It is easy to frame the whole space as a contest between models, prompts, and editors. Those things are visible, easy to compare, and fun to optimize. But the deeper problem starts after the code is generated, when the team has to answer a much harder question:
What is the actual state of this change?
If the answer depends on local diffs, generated files, tests, CI, review decisions, and undocumented conventions, then the problem is no longer just “can the model write code?” It becomes “can the workflow reconcile all the systems that now participate in code delivery?”
That is a software engineering problem in the old-fashioned sense. It is about boundaries, validation, reliability, and operational discipline.
It is also why AI and modern application architecture are starting to converge in an interesting way. Whether the domain is AI workflows, backend systems, or even the overlap between AI and Web3 that I touched on in AI and Web3 Are Merging. Most People Have No Idea What It Means for Them., the same question keeps coming back:
Where does the real state live, and which system is responsible for proving it is coherent?
That is the question that matters.
The teams that benefit most from AI coding will not be the teams with the cleverest prompts
They will be the teams with the strongest workflow boundaries.
They will be the teams whose repositories know how to validate themselves, whose CI catches drift early, whose tests reflect real behavior, and whose review process can reconstruct what actually changed instead of trusting that the generated diff “looks right.”
The prompt still matters.
The model still matters.
But once AI starts writing real production code, the bottleneck is no longer code generation alone. It is the workflow’s ability to reconcile generated changes with the messy, distributed reality of repository state.
That is why AI coding is not mainly a prompt problem.
It is a state problem.
And the teams that understand that early will build much more reliable workflows than the ones still trying to solve everything inside the prompt box.
